verdict: PASS — all checks passed. Reading is 1373 words (within 1200–1600 range), 7 min at 200 wpm (within 6–8 min range). Primary resource [1] cited at lines 11, 22, 52, 90, 112. All six fixed sections present in correct order with no YAML frontmatter. Ten sampled assertions all trace to corpus. No forward topic references. Beginner readability is strong: terms defined on first use (algorithm line 11, algorithmic thinking line 52, edge cases line 40), concrete analogies precede abstractions throughout, no jargon dumps, no hedging language. Formatting is clean: procedures as lists, bold-term definitions throughout, inline examples in Key Concepts (cooking timer, 'do the right thing', Version A/B exam), no wall-of-text paragraphs (longest is 5 sentences in Overview). No assessment references. Scope stays within the headline — GPS and medical protocol examples are illustrative only, not teaching routing algorithms or clinical practice. No gold example file found in .claude/references/gold-examples/reading/; evaluation made against written standards in artifact-mix.json and SKILL.md.

format_discipline.notes: First non-empty line is correct H1. reading.meta.json exists, parses, contains word_count=1373, reading_minutes=7, resources_cited=[1,2,3]. Six fixed sections in correct order.

citations.notes: [1] Bouras cited 5 times; [2] Wikipedia cited at lines 40, 96; [3] Purdue cited at lines 98, 116. Bibliography at lines 112-116 matches all inline markers exactly.

no_forward_references.notes: No references to topics 1.10, 1.11, or any later concept. Backward references to 1.2 (determinism) and 1.7 (pseudocode) and 1.8 (flowcharts) are all valid prior-topic concepts. The corpus introduction mentions 1.10/1.11 as deferred topics but the reading itself correctly omits those forward pointers.

sample_trace.assertion: algorithm is finite, unambiguous sequence with defined inputs producing a result
three failure modes: missing steps, vague steps, wrong order
'do the right thing' machine example — three bad outcomes: halt, arbitrary, inconsistent
algorithmic thinking is not a programming skill and not mathematics
four qualities: precise, complete, ordered, decisive
library late-fee worked example with five algorithmic steps
GPS navigation selects lowest-cost path — same route every time
medical protocols replace judgment with defined rules for consistency
Version A/B exam pass/fail — score >= 50 decision rule
vagueness destroys determinism — same input, different outputs

faithfulness.notes: All 10 sampled assertions have clear corpus provenance. No new claims introduced by the reading.

beginner_readability.notes: 'algorithm' defined with bold + plain meaning at line 11. 'algorithmic thinking' defined at line 52. 'determinism' referenced as 'the property you met in topic 1.2' — valid backward reference. 'edge cases' defined inline at line 40 as 'unusual but valid inputs'. Overview opens with a concrete machine/recipe analogy before introducing the formal concept. No hedging language found. Average sentence length is well under 25 words throughout.

formatting.notes: Overview is 5 sentences (at boundary, acceptable). Longest body paragraph (why vagueness breaks computation) is 5 sentences. Six-step checklist is a numbered list (lines 71-77). Three failure modes shown as table (lines 24-29). Four algorithmic thinking qualities as bullet list (lines 54-57). Inline examples in Key Concepts: cooking timer (line 18), 'do the right thing' (lines 34-38), Version A/B diagram with exam pass/fail (lines 59-66). Bold-term definitions: algorithm (line 11), Finite/Unambiguous/Defined inputs/Result (lines 13-16), Algorithmic thinking (line 52), Precise/Complete/Ordered/Decisive (lines 54-57).

assessment_references.notes: No mention of assessment IDs, due weeks, or grade weights.

scope.notes: GPS and medical protocol examples used as illustrative instances of 'precise decision rule applied consistently' — not teaching routing algorithms or clinical protocols as subjects. The 'edge cases' concept introduced at line 40 is grounded in the corpus (§5.4) and used to illustrate why vagueness breaks computation rather than as a standalone topic. No concepts from 1.10 (algorithms in everyday life) or 1.11 (formal algorithm properties) are taught; both are absent from the reading. Deferred concepts are not named.
